Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The game is definitively in GAME_OVER state. After 35 actions (mostly oscillating LEFT/RIGHT with no progress), the level failed. The last action was RESET which restored the board but didn't clear the game_over flag. Score: 0. No further actions will be taken per the explicit instruction to stop when game_over is detected.
- Plan: Move RIGHT to collect the yellow target, then check if the level completes.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 81 actions; a strong score needs about 21 or fewer. You are 3.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The board changed (board_changed: true) but the level didn't complete. Let me check what actually changed. The player is still in the same position. Maybe clicking the yellow target didn't work. Let me look at the current state more carefully.
Looking at the images, they all look the same - the blue player is next to the yellow target in the alcove. The step counter went from 80 to 81.
Let me check if the yellow target is still there or if something changed. I should use segmentation to see what objects exist now vs before.
Actually, I notice the board_changed is true but the images look identical. Maybe the change was just the step counter in the HUD. Let me check the segmentation to see if the yellow target is still there.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed - look at segmentation to see if yellow target is still there
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ('Y', 'b'):
        print(f"Color={n['color']}, id={n['id']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The yellow target is still there. The MOUSE click didn't collect it. The player is still stuck in the alcove. Let me think about this differently.
Looking at the images, the player (blue) is in a small alcove with a yellow target to its right. The player can't move RIGHT because the yellow target is blocking. But the target is supposed to be collected somehow.
Wait - maybe I need to move the player INTO the target's position. But RIGHT doesn't work. Maybe I need to use a different action. Let me try ACTION7 which is one of the valid actions.
Actually, let me reconsider. The player is at boundary [[37, 51], [40, 51], [39, 50], [38, 50]] and the yellow is at [[38, 52], [39, 52]]. The player is adjacent to the yellow target. 
Let me look at the ASCII more carefully to understand the layout. Maybe I need to move LEFT first to get out of the alcove, then navigate differently.
Actually wait - looking at the ASCII from before:
Row 38: 'SSSSBbbYBBBBB'
- col 45-48: S (sky blue)
- col 49: B (black)
- col 50-51: b (player)
- col 52: Y (yellow)
The player is at col 50-51, and to the left is col 49 which is B (black). So LEFT is blocked too at row 38-39.
But at row 37 and 40, the player is only at col 51, and to the left at col 50 is B (black). So the player is completely surrounded by black walls except for the yellow target to the right.
This is a dead end. The player can't move anywhere with LEFT/RIGHT. Maybe ACTION7 does something special? Or maybe I need to click on something else with MOUSE?
Let me try ACTION7 to see what it does.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player is stuck in alcove, can't move LEFT or RIGHT. Try ACTION7
action(['ACTION7'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Let me try LEFT to see if the player can move.
